text: The surgeon performs adnexal dissection on the right side during a laparoscopic hysterectomy, with visible omentum in the abdominal cavity due to the patient's obesity. The left side round ligament is dissected using a 37mm instrument.

The surgeon identifies the left fallopian tube and ovarian ligament, using ipsilateral and contralateral ports for access. The anterior leaf of the broad ligament is opened with anterior traction applied by a grasper, while a harmonic device opens the UV fold. The uterus is rotated from five o'clock to seven o'clock during the procedure. A commercially available blunt dissection tool is used over the colpotomizer to separate the bladder, revealing the shiny cervical vaginal fascia as the bladder is pushed upwards.

The surgeon coagulates the uterine artery on the right side using a ligature, ensuring the artery is at a right angle for effective coagulation. Saline irrigation is applied to enhance the ligature's effect. Both uterine arteries are coagulated, and the left side is cut. The harmonic device performs a full circle colpotomy, with its tip positioned within the colpotomizer ring. The uterus is separated from the vagina and removed using a tenaculum attached to the colpotomizer, ensuring no omentum or rectum is caught during extraction.

Following the removal of the uterus, the surgeon performs a salpingo-oophorectomy with anteromedial traction using a ligature device. The excised tissue is pushed into the vagina for removal. On the left side, care is taken due to the presence of the sigmoid colon, which may require mobilization. The vaginal vault is then closed using a Western knot technique, ensuring full thickness of the anterior and posterior vaginal walls is included. Multiple knots are applied to secure the closure.
